Module 1: First Steps (The Essentials).title: Week 2: Greetings – Survival Scenario: Ordering a Coffee
Week 3: Naming Things – The Nouns & Genders
Week 4: The Case System – Survival Scenario: Supermarket
MILESTONE 1: I can order coffee, introduce myself, and shop for essentials.

Module 2: Expanding Your World (Intermed.title: Week 5: Action – Introduction to Verbs & Groups
Week 6: People & Ownership – Personal & Possessive Pronouns
MILESTONE 2: I can describe my day, talk about my family, and describe objects.

Module 3: Advanced Communication (Fluenc.title: Week 9: Memory – Past Conjugation & Travel Stories
Week 10: Space – The Prepositions & Giving Directions
Week 12: Real World – Survival Scenario: The Airport & Hotel
MILESTONE 3: I can travel through Greece, tell stories, and have basic debates.

pronouns.title: Week 6: People & Ownership – Personal & Possessive Pronouns
pronouns.content: Intro to Pronouns Pronouns are words used in place of nouns to avoid repetition and make speech more fluid. Modern Greek has several categories of pronouns, most of which decline based on gender, number, and case—just like the nouns they replace. Personal Pronouns I, you, he, she, it... Possessive Pronouns My, your, his, hers... Demonstrative Pronouns This, that, these, those... Relative Pronouns Who, which, that... Key Rule: Omission In Modern Greek, subject pronouns (I, you, we) are often omitted because the verb ending already tells us who is performing the action. We only use them for emphasis or clarity. Διαβάζω. (I read) vs Εγώ διαβάζω. ( I [emphasized] read)

basics.title: Week 1: Foundations – The Alphabet & Reading
basics.content: The Alphabet The Greek alphabet consists of 24 distinct graphemes. It is the foundation of Western writing. Mastery of these symbols is the first step to literacy. Letter Name Sound Example Α α Alpha [a] hat Β β Beta [v] vase Γ γ Gamma [γ] rendez-vous Δ δ Delta [δ] than Ε ε Epsilon [e] pen Ζ ζ Zeta [z] zoo Η η Eta [i] tip Θ θ Theta [θ] thin Ι ι Iota [i] tip Κ κ Kappa [k] book Λ λ Lambda [l] like Μ μ Mu [m] make Ν ν Nu [n] noise Ξ ξ Xi [ks] ox Ο ο Omicron [o] top Π π Pi [p] stop Ρ ρ Rho [r] Roberto Σ σ/ς Sigma [s] see Τ τ Tau [t] spot Υ υ Upsilon [i] tip Φ φ Phi [f] fish Χ χ Chi [h] loch Ψ ψ Psi [ps] stops Ω ω Omega [o] top Orthographic Notes Case Sensitivity : Proper nouns and sentence starters are capitalized. Question Mark : Greek uses a semicolon (;) as a question mark. Sigma : Use σ normally, but ς at the end of a word.

auxiliary-verbs.title: Auxiliary Verbs
auxiliary-verbs.content: Auxiliary Verbs Auxiliary verbs are "helper" verbs used to form complex tenses. The two most important auxiliary verbs in Greek are είμαι (to be) and έχω (to have). 1. To Be (Είμαι) Person Present (I am) Past (I was) Εγώ είμαι ήμουν(α) Εσύ είσαι ήσουν(α) Αυτός/ή/ό είναι ήταν(ε) Εμείς είμαστε ήμασταν Εσείς είστε ήσασταν Αυτοί/ές/ά είναι ήταν(ε) 2. To Have (Έχω) Person Present (I have) Past (I had) Εγώ έχω είχα Εσύ έχεις είχες Αυτός/ή/ό έχει είχε Εμείς έχουμε είχαμε Εσείς έχετε είχατε Αυτοί/ές/ά έχουν(ε) είχαν(ε) Did you know? The verb έχω is used exactly like in English to form the perfect tenses. Example: Έχω διαβάσει. (I have read).

possessive-pronouns.title: possessive-pronouns
possessive-pronouns.content: Possessive Pronouns In Greek, possession is usually expressed using a "weak" form of the personal pronoun that follows the noun. To express "own" or for stronger emphasis, we use the word δικός . 1. The Weak Forms (My, Your, His...) These short words always follow the noun and never change their form based on the noun's case—they only match the person and number of the owner. Singular Owner μου (my) σου (your) του / της / του (his / her / its) Plural Owner μας (our) σας (your [pl]) τους (their) Examples το βιβλίο μου (my book) το μολύβι του (his pencil) το σκυλί τους (their dog) 2. Emphatic Possession (δικός) The word δικός, δική, δικό acts like an adjective and is used to say "mine" or "my own". It must match the gender of the object being possessed. το δικό μου my own / mineτο δικό σου your own / yoursτο δικό του his own / his Pro-Tip: The Article When using δικός , you often need an article before it. Think of it like saying "the mine own". Example: Έχω το δικό μου αυτοκίνητο. (I have my own car).

read.title: Learn to Read (Intensive Practice)
read.content: Learn to Read Reading Greek is a process of assembling phonetic blocks (syllables). Unlike English, Greek is highly phonetic—once you know the sound of a letter or a "double letter", it almost always sounds the same. Syllabic Assembly Follow these examples to see how sounds combine to form words. μα + μάμαμά mama / mother πα + τέ + ραςπατέρας pateras / father ου + ρα + νόςουρανός ouranos / sky τζά + κιτζάκι tzaki / fireplace Key Takeaway Treat every word as a series of distinct sounds. Notice how the accent mark dictates where the voice rises. Without the accent mark, we wouldn't know which syllable to emphasize!

verbs.title: Week 5: Action – Introduction to Verbs & Groups
verbs.content: Introduction to Verbs In Ancient and Modern Greek, verbs are the heartbeat of the sentence. Most regular verbs follow predictable patterns. Explore the Present Indicative Active below. The Anatomy of a Greek Verb Greek verbs have a stem and an ending . The stem carries the meaning (e.g., λύ- means "loose"), while the ending tells us who is performing the action. -ω : 1st Person Singular (I) -εις : 2nd Person Singular (You) -ει : 3rd Person Singular (He/She/It)

social-expressions.title: Week 3: Connections – Social Expressions
social-expressions.content: Social Expressions This lesson covers common expressions used in social settings, including answering the phone and social events. On the Phone Εμπρός; (Hello? - answering) Ναι; (Yes? / Hello?) Ποιος είναι; (Who is it?) Ένα λεπτό. (One minute.) Congratulations & Wishes Χρόνια Πολλά! (Happy Birthday/Anniversary!) Συγχαρητήρια! (Congratulations!) Καλή επιτυχία! (Good luck!) Περαστικά! (Get well soon!) Drinks & Food Στην υγειά μας! (Cheers!) Γειά μας! (Cheers!) Καλή όρεξη! (Bon Appétit!) Cultural Note Χρόνια Πολλά is the most common wish in Greece. It is used for birthdays, name days, weddings, Christmas, Easter, and almost every national holiday!

adjectives.title: Week 8: Descriptions – The Adjectives
adjectives.content: The Adjectives In Greek, adjectives must agree with the noun they modify in gender , number , and case . This means that a single adjective like "good" can have many different forms. Gender Agreement Every adjective has three genders to match whatever noun it describes. Ο καλ ός πατέρας The good father (Masc)Η καλ ή μητέρα The good mother (Fem)Το καλ ό παιδί The good child (Neut) Common Adjective Patterns Most adjectives follow the -ος, -η, -ο pattern or the -ος, -α, -ο pattern. Root Masculine Feminine Neuter Good καλός καλή καλό Tall ψηλός ψηλή ψηλό Small μικρός μικρή μικρό Beautiful ωραίος ωραία ωραίο Declension Rule Adjectives are declined exactly like the nouns of the same gender. A masculine adjective in -ος declines exactly like a masculine noun in -ος .

personal-pronouns.title: personal-pronouns
personal-pronouns.content: Personal Pronouns The personal pronouns in Greek change based on the person (1st, 2nd, 3rd) and their function in the sentence (Subject vs. Object). 1. Subject Pronouns (Nominative) These are used to represent the person performing the action. Person Singular Plural 1st (I / We) εγώ εμείς 2nd (You) εσύ εσείς 3rd (He/She/It) αυτός (M) αυτή (F) αυτό (N) αυτοί (M) αυτές (F) αυτά (N) 2. Weak vs. Strong Forms Greek has two ways to say things like "me" or "him". Strong forms are for emphasis, while Weak forms (clitics) are used in normal speech and usually attached to the verb. Example Strong (Emphatic) Weak (Normal) Me εμένα με You εσένα σε Him αυτόν τον Her αυτή(ν) τη(ν) Usage in Sentences Σε βλέπω. (I see you - Normal) Βλέπω εσένα . (I see YOU - Emphasized)

interrogative-pronouns.title: Interrogative Pronouns
interrogative-pronouns.content: Interrogative Pronouns Interrogative pronouns are used to ask questions. In Greek, almost all interrogative pronouns carry an accent mark to distinguish them from their non-interrogative counterparts. Who / Which (ποιος, ποια, ποιο) Used to ask about identity. It declines like an adjective. Ποιος είναι στην πόρτα; ( Who is at the door?) How much / many (πόσος, πόση, πόσο) Used to ask about quantity. Πόσο κάνει; ( How much does it cost?) What (τι) The universal "what". It is indeclinable (stays the same). Τι κάνεις; ( What are you doing?) Accentuation Note In Modern Greek, one of the few words that always keep their accent even when they are single-syllable are interrogative words (πώς, πού, τι can be an exception but usually keeps the context). The accent tells the reader "this is a question".

vowels-consonants.title: Week 1: Vowels and Consonants
vowels-consonants.content: Vowels and Consonants Understanding the classification of Greek letters into vowels and consonants is the final step before mastering the stress system. The 7 Vowels (Φωνήεντα) Greek has seven vowels, although some represent the same sound due to historical phonetic evolution. Α α [a] Ε ε [e] Η η [i] Ι ι [i] Υ υ [i] Ο ο [o] Ω ω [o] Did you know? In Modern Greek, η, ι, υ all make the exact same "ee" sound. This is called iotacism . The 17 Consonants (Σύμφωνα) Consonants are divided into categories based on how they are produced (voiced vs. voiceless), but for now, remember their forms: Β, Γ, Δ, Ζ, Θ, Κ, Λ, Μ, Ν, Ξ, Π, Ρ, Σ, Τ, Φ, Χ, Ψ The Importance of Vowels Vowels are the carriers of the accent . In Lesson 5, you will learn that the accent mark can only be placed above one of these seven vowels.

nouns.title: Week 3: Naming Things – The Nouns & Genders
nouns.content: The Nouns Nouns in Modern Greek (Τα Ουσιαστικά) are complex entities governed by gender, case, and number. Gender is a fundamental property. The Three Genders Masculine (Ο) ο δρόμος (street) άντρας (man) χάρτης (map) Feminine (Η) η ώρα (hour) ζώνη (belt) γυναίκα (woman) Neuter (Το) το βιβλίο (book) παιδί (child) πράγμα (thing) Case Study Ο Γιάννης μένει με τον Πέτρο . John lives with Peter. Ο Πέτρος μένει με τον Γιάννη . Peter lives with John. The Golden Rule Never learn a noun in isolation. Always learn it alongside its definite article (ο, η, το).

verb-to-be.title: Week 2: Existence – The Verb 'To Be' (Είμαι)
verb-to-be.content: The Verb 'To Be' (Είμαι) The verb 'to be' is arguably the most important verb in any language. In Greek, it is irregular but follows a consistent pattern of endings. 1. Present Indicative Person Greek English I am Εγώ είμαι I am You are Εσύ είσαι You are He/She/It is Αυτός/ή/ό είναι He/She/It is We are Εμείς είμαστε We are You (pl/formal) Εσείς είστε You are They are Αυτοί/ές/ά είναι They are 2. Interaction: Translation Lab Click the Greek phrases below to toggle their English meaning. Observe how the verb changes. Είμαι καλά Είσαι έτοιμος; Είμαστε εδώ

feminine-declension.title: Week 7: Gender Focus – Feminine Declension
feminine-declension.content: Feminine Declension Feminine nouns in Greek typically end in -α or -η . Unlike masculine nouns, they are generally very consistent in their declension patterns. 1. Nouns in -α (η μητέρα - the mother) Case Singular Plural Nom η μητέρ α οι μητέρ ες Gen της μητέρ ας των μητέρ ων Acc τη(ν) μητέρ α τις μητέρ ες 2. Nouns in -η (η ζωή - the life) Case Singular Plural Nom η ζω ή οι ζω ές Gen της ζω ής των ζω ών Acc τη(ν) ζω ή τις ζω ές The Special "Mama" Case Some feminine nouns (often kinship terms or loanwords) ending in an accented -ά follow a slightly different plural pattern: η μαμά → οι μαμά δες η γιαγιά → οι γιαγιά δες

interjections.title: Interjections
interjections.content: The Interjections Interjections (Επιφωνήματα) are words that express sudden emotions or reactions. They are often used in spoken Greek to add color and feeling to a conversation. Μπράβο!Bravo! / Well done! Α μπά!No way! / Don't tell me! Άντε! / Άιντε!Come on! / Let's go! Αχ!Oh! / Ah! (pain or sigh) Μακάρι!I wish! / Hopefully! Αλίμονο!Alas! / Unfortunately! Εύγε!Excellent! / Bravo! Cultural Highlight: "Άντε" Άντε is one of the most common words in Greece. Depending on the tone, it can mean "Come on", "Let's go", "Hurry up", or even "Get out of here!" (in disbelief).

accents.title: Accents (Τονισμός)
accents.content: Accents (Τονισμός) In Modern Greek, the accent mark (ο τόνος) is a crucial orthographic feature that indicates which syllable of a word is stressed. Unlike English, where stress patterns can be ambiguous, Greek uses a visible mark to ensure correct pronunciation. The Fundamental Rules Target: The accent goes only on top of vowels. Position: Only the last three syllables of a word can carry an accent. Monosyllables: Words with only one syllable generally do not take an accent (with a few grammatical exceptions). Examples of Syllabic Stress πατέρας (pa-TÉ-ras) - father μητέρα (mi-TÉ-ra) - mother αυτοκίνητο (af-to-KÍ-ni-to) - car οικογένεια (i-ko-YÉ-ni-a) - family Why Accents Matter The placement of the accent can change the entire meaning of a word. For example, νόμος (nómos) means "law", while νομός (nomós) means "prefecture" or "district".

tenses.title: Week 9: Time – Verb Tenses Overview
tenses.content: Verb Tenses Greek verb tenses are divided based on when the action happens (Past, Present, Future) and how it happens (Continuous vs. Simple/Instant). Present (Ενεστώτας) παίζω I am playing / I play Future (Μέλλοντας) θα παίζω I will be playing (continuous)θα παίξω I will play (once) Past (Παρελθόν) έπαιζα I was playing (Imperfect)έπαιξα I played (Aorist) Perfect (Παρακείμενος) έχω παίξει I have played The Aspect Rule In Greek, the most important distinction is between Continuous (actions that happen repeatedly or over time) and Simple (actions that happen once). This distinction is reflected in most tenses!

genders.title: The Genders (Deep Dive)
genders.content: The Genders Every Greek noun belongs to one of three grammatical categories: Masculine, Feminine, or Neuter. 01. Masculine (Ο) Ends generally in -ος, -as, or -ης. Examples: ο δρόμος (street), ο πατέρας (father). 02. Feminine (Η) Ends generally in -η or -a. Examples: η μητέρα (mother), η καρδιά (heart). 03. Neuter (Το) Ends generally in -o, -ι, or -μα. Examples: το παιδί (child), το σπίτι (house). Note on Ambiguity While most -ος nouns are masculine, some are feminine (η οδός). The article is your only fail-safe guide.

relative-pronouns.title: Week 11: Elaborating – Relative Pronouns
relative-pronouns.content: Relative Pronouns Relative pronouns are used to connect clauses or refer back to a noun previously mentioned. The most common one is the indeclinable που . που That / Which / Who The universal relative pronoun. It never changes its form. Αγόρασα το βιβλίο που είδα. (I bought the book that I saw.) ό,τι Whatever / What Used to refer to an unspecified thing or sentence. Πάρε ό,τι θέλεις. (Take whatever you want.) όποιος, -α, -ο Whoever / Whichever Declines like an adjective. Όποιος κλέβει... ( Whoever steals...) όσος, -η, -ο As much as Indicates quantity. Δώσε μου όσα λεφτά θέλεις. (Give me as much money as you want.) Spelling Alert: που vs πού Don't confuse the relative pronoun που (no accent) with the interrogative πού (with accent). Το σπίτι που μένω. (The house that I live in.) Πού μένεις; ( Where do you live?)

particles.title: The Particles
particles.content: The Particles Particles (Μόρια) are small, indeclinable words that don't fit easily into other categories but play a vital role in Greek syntax, specifically in forming future tenses, subjunctive moods, and expressing emphasis. θα - Future marker (will) να - Subjunctive marker (to / let) ας - Exhortative marker (let / let it be) μα - Emphatic "but" or "though" Usage Examples Θα πάω. (I will go.) Θέλω να πάω. (I want to go.) Ας πάμε! ( Let's go!) Congratulations! You have reached the end of the Formal Grammar section of the Modern Greek curriculum. You now have a solid foundation in phonology, morphology, and basic syntax. Next, dive into the Practical Phrases and Dialogues to see all this grammar in action!

prepositions.title: Week 10: Space – The Prepositions & Giving Directions
prepositions.content: The Prepositions Prepositions (Προθέσεις) are small, indeclinable words that usually come before a noun or pronoun to show relationship in space, time, or manner. In Modern Greek, nouns following a preposition are almost always in the Accusative case. Survival Scenario: Giving Directions (Δίνοντας Οδηγίες) Use prepositions to find your way around a Greek city! Πάω στο κέντρο. (I am going to the center.) Πήγαινε προς την πλατεία. (Go towards the square.) Το ξενοδοχείο είναι δίπλα στην εκκλησία. (The hotel is next to the church.) Είμαι από την Αθήνα. (I am from Athens.) με with σε in / to / at για for / about από from / by / than χωρίς / δίχως without μετά after προς towards αντί (για) instead (of) Examples in Action Ήρθα από τη δουλειά. (I came from work.) Πήγα στο θέατρο με την Μαρία. (I went to the theater with Maria.) Έφυγα χωρίς λεφτά. (I left without money.) Για σένα. ( For you.) Grammar Note: σε + το = στο The preposition σε (to/in) almost always merges with the definite article that follows it: σε + τον = στον σε + την = στην σε + το = στο

past-conjugation.title: Week 9: Memory – Past Conjugation & Travel Stories
past-conjugation.content: Past Tense Conjugation In Modern Greek, there are two main past tenses: the Imperfect (actions that were happening) and the Aorist (actions that happened once). They use different stems and endings. Survival Scenario: Travel Stories (Ιστορίες Ταξιδιού) Use the Aorist to tell what happened during your trip! Πήγα στην Ελλάδα. (I went to Greece.) Έφαγα σουβλάκι. (I ate souvlaki.) Ήταν υπέροχα! (It was wonderful!) Είδα την Ακρόπολη. (I saw the Acropolis.) Imperfect (Παρατατικός) "I was playing" Εγώ έπαιζ-α Εσύ έπαιζ-ες Αυτός έπαιζ-ε Εμείς παίζ-αμε Εσείς παίζ-ατε Αυτοί έπαιζ-αν Aorist (Αόριστος) "I played" Εγώ έπαιξ-α Εσύ έπαιξ-ες Αυτός έπαιξ-ε Εμείς παίξ-αμε Εσείς παίξ-ατε Αυτοί έπαιξ-αν The Augment (Το Πρόσφυμα) Did you notice the extra έ- at the beginning of some forms? This is called the Augment . It is added to past tenses when the verb has two syllables, making it three. In plural forms like παίζαμε , the stress moves, and the augment usually disappears.

declension.title: Week 6: Mastery – The Declension (Intro)
declension.content: The Declension In English, words like "friend" usually stay the same whether they are the subject or the object of a sentence. In Greek, nouns are inflected . This means they change their ending based on their grammatical role. This process is called Declension . The "Flexing" Language Think of Greek as a "flexing" language. Just as you flex a muscle, words "flex" their endings to signal their function. If a word is the "doer" (subject), it has one ending; if it's the "receiver" (object), it has another. What gets declined? In most Greek sentences, the following elements must agree and decline together: Articles: ο, η, το... Nouns: πατέρας, μητέρα... Adjectives: καλός, μεγάλη... Pronouns: αυτός, εγώ... Standard vs. Irregular Most nouns follow predictable patterns (Regular Nouns). However, some nouns are "indeclinable" (don't change) or follow their own unique rules. We will focus on the regular patterns first.

punctuation.title: Punctuation Marks
punctuation.content: Punctuation Marks Greek punctuation is very similar to English, but there are two critical differences that often confuse beginners: the question mark and the upper point . . Full Stop (Τελεία) Functions exactly as in English. Ends a sentence. , Comma (Κόμμα) Functions exactly as in English. Separates clauses. ; Question Mark (Ερωτηματικό) In Greek, the semicolon character is the question mark. Τι κάνεις; (How are you?) · Ano Teleia (Άνω Τελεία) The "Upper Point" functions like a colon or a semicolon in English. It is a dot placed at the top of the line. Comparison Summary Function English Greek End of Sentence . . Question ? ; Pause/List ; or : · Pro Tip: When typing in Greek, the semicolon key (usually to the right of 'L') produces the Greek question mark ; .

indefinite-pronouns.title: Indefinite Pronouns
indefinite-pronouns.content: Indefinite Pronouns Indefinite pronouns refer to people or things in a non-specific way. Greek has a rich set of these pronouns, some of which are declinable and others which are stable (indeclinable). κάποιος, -α, -ο Somebody / Someone Refering to an unknown specific person. κάτι Something Indeclinable. Used for unspecified things. κάθε / καθένας Each / Every κάθε is indeclinable; καθένας declines like ένας . κανένας / καμία / κανένα Nobody / No one Also means "anyone" in questions. τίποτα Nothing / Anything Indeclinable. Used in negative sentences. άλλος, -η, -ο Another / Other Declines like a standard adjective. Usage in Sentences Κάποιος είναι εκεί. ( Someone is there.) Δεν θέλω τίποτα . (I don't want anything/nothing .) Κάθε μέρα. ( Every day.)

demonstrative-pronouns.title: Week 11: Specifics – Demonstrative & Interrogative Pronouns
demonstrative-pronouns.content: Demonstrative Pronouns Demonstrative pronouns are used to point to specific objects or people, indicating their relative distance from the speaker. αυτός, αυτή, αυτό This (Close by) Used for things near the speaker. Αυτός είναι ο καφές μου. This is my coffee. εκείνος, εκείνη, εκείνο That (Far away) Used for things distant from the speaker. Εκείνο το σπίτι. That house. τέτοιος, τέτοια, τέτοιο Such Used to indicate quality or type. Δεν είναι τέτοιος άνθρωπος. He is not such a man. τόσος, τόση, τόσο So much / many Used to indicate quantity or degree. Πήγαμε τόσο μακριά. We went so far. Grammatical Note When a demonstrative pronoun is used with a noun, the noun must also have an article . Example: Αυτός ο σκύλος (This [the] dog).

conjunctions.title: Week 10: Complex Sentences – The Conjunctions
conjunctions.content: The Conjunctions Conjunctions (Σύνδεσμοι) are words used to connect sentences, phrases, or individual words. They are essential for building complex thoughts and flowing conversation. Connecting (Συμπλεκτικοί) και / κι and ούτε / μήτε neither / nor ή ... ή either ... or Contrasting (Αντιθετικοί) αλλά / μα but όμως / ωστόσο however / yet αν και although ενώ while Causal (Αιτιολογικοί) γιατί because / why επειδή since / as διότι because (formal) Time (Χρονικοί) όταν when πριν before αφού after / since μόλις as soon as Result (Συμπερασματικοί) άρα / επομένως therefore λοιπόν so / well ώστε so that Purpose / Condition να / για να to / so that αν / εάν if μήπως maybe / lest Common Patterns Θέλω να πάω αλλά δεν μπορώ. (I want to go but I cannot.) Είναι καλό γιατί είναι φθηνό. (It is good because it is cheap.)

reflexive-pronouns.title: Reflexive Pronouns
reflexive-pronouns.content: Reflexive Pronouns Reflexive pronouns are used when the subject and the object of a verb are the same person. In Greek, we use the phrase ο εαυτός μου (my self). 1st Person ο εαυτός μου myself 2nd Person ο εαυτός σου yourself 3rd Person ο εαυτός του / της himself / herself Plural Reflexives In the plural, the word "self" (εαυτός) can either stay singular or become plural (εαυτοί). Person Singular (Self) Plural (Selves) 1st (Ourselves) τον εαυτό μας τους εαυτούς μας 2nd (Yourselves) τον εαυτό σας τους εαυτούς σας 3rd (Themselves) τον εαυτό τους τους εαυτούς τους Common Usage Reflexive pronouns are usually used as objects. Example: Σκέφτομαι τον εαυτό μου. (I am thinking of myself).

articles-declension.title: Articles Declension (Reference)
articles-declension.content: Articles Declension Just like nouns, the Greek definite article ("the") must be declined to match the case of the noun it describes. This is one of the most important patterns to memorize, as it often provides the clearest clue to a noun's case. Case Masculine Feminine Neuter Singular (Ενικός) Nom ο η το Gen του της του Acc το(ν) τη(ν) το Plural (Πληθυντικός) Nom οι οι τα Gen των των των Acc τους τις τα Patterns to Watch Shared Genitive: Masculine and Neuter share the same Genitive Singular (του). Universal Genitive Plural: The Genitive Plural is always των for every single gender and noun in the Greek language! Shared Plural Nom: Masculine and Feminine share the same Nominative Plural (οι).

coming-soon.title: Coming Soon
coming-soon.content: Coming Soon This lesson is currently being written. The curriculum is released in stages to ensure quality. ← Back to Curriculum

dialogues-travel.title: Week 12: Real World – Survival Scenario: The Airport & Hotel
dialogues-travel.content: Travel Dialogues When traveling in Greece, knowing how to ask for directions and handle common travel situations is essential. Survival Scenario: The Airport & Hotel (Το Αεροδρόμιο και το Ξενοδοχείο) Handle your arrival in Greece like a native! Έχω μια κράτηση. (I have a reservation.) Πού είναι οι αποσκευές; (Where is the luggage?) Πού είναι η πύλη; (Where is the gate?) Έχετε κλιματισμό; (Do you have air conditioning?) Asking Where Something Is Πού είναι η τουαλέτα; (Where is the bathroom?) Πού είναι η στάση; (Where is the bus stop?) Πού είναι η έξοδος; (Where is the exit?) Asking How to Get Somewhere Πώς πάω στην πλατεία; (How do I get to the square?) Πώς πάω στην Ακρόπολη; (How do I get to the Acropolis?) Direction Vocabulary Ευθεία (Straight) Αριστερά (Left) Δεξιά (Right) Εδώ / Εκεί (Here / There) Travel Tip In Greece, a nod of the head upward often means "No" (Όχι), while a nod downward means "Yes" (Ναι). This can be confusing for visitors at first!

masculine-declension.title: Week 7: Gender Focus – Masculine Declension
masculine-declension.content: Masculine Declension Masculine nouns in Greek are the most common group to exhibit clear inflectional changes. Most masculine nouns end in -ος , -ας , or -ης . 1. Nouns in -ος (ο φίλος - the friend) Case Singular Plural Nom ο φίλ ος οι φίλ οι Gen του φίλ ου των φίλ ων Acc το(ν) φίλ ο τους φίλ ους 2. Nouns in -ας (ο πατέρας - the father) Case Singular Plural Nom ο πατέρ ας οι πατέρ ες Gen του πατέρ α των πατέρ ων Acc το(ν) πατέρ α τους πατέρ ες 3. Nouns in -ης (ο μαθητής - the student) Case Singular Plural Nom ο μαθητ ής οι μαθητ ές Gen του μαθητ ή των μαθητ ών Acc το(ν) μαθητ ή τους μαθητ ές Pro-Tip: The "S" Rule Notice how nouns in -ας and -ης simply drop the -ς in the Genitive and Accusative cases. This makes them much easier to memorize than the -ος group!

degrees-adjectives.title: Degrees of Adjectives
degrees-adjectives.content: Degrees of Adjectives In Greek, adjectives change their form to indicate intensity. There are three degrees: Positive (tall), Comparative (taller), and Superlative (tallest). 1. The Positive Degree (Θετικός) This is the base form of the adjective as learned in the previous lesson. ωραίος (beautiful) καλός (good) 2. The Comparative Degree (Συγκριτικός) There are two ways to form the comparative in Greek: Method A: Using "πιο" Simply place the word πιο (more) before the adjective. This works for almost all adjectives. πιο ωραίος (more beautiful / handsomer) Method B: Using Suffixes Add the suffix -ότερος to the root of the adjective. ωραι ότερος (more beautiful) 3. The Superlative Degree (Υπεθετικός) Type Formation Example Relative Article + "πιο" + Adj ο πιο ωραίος (the most beautiful) Absolute Add -ότατος ωραι ότατος (very beautiful) Common Irregulars Some adjectives have unique comparative forms: καλός → καλύτερος (better) μεγάλος → μεγαλύτερος (bigger)

double-letters.title: Week 1: Sounds – Double Letters
double-letters.content: Double Letters In Modern Greek, certain combinations of two letters are used to represent a single sound. These are called digraphs or "double letters". They are essential for correct pronunciation and spelling. Double Vowels (Diphthongs) Combination Sound Example Translation αι [e] (as in 'let') αίμα blood ει, οι, υι [i] (as in 'seen') είμαι / οικογένεια I am / family ου [u] (as in 'boot') ουρανός sky The Special Cases: αυ & ευ These combinations change their sound based on the letter that follows them. Sound like [av/ev] before vowels or "voiced" consonants (β, γ, δ, ζ, λ, μ, ν, ρ). Sound like [af/ef] before "voiceless" consonants (θ, κ, ξ, π, σ, τ, φ, χ, ψ). Double Consonants Combination Sound Example Translation μπ [b] (beginning of word) μπαμπάς dad ντ [d] (beginning of word) ντύνω to dress γκ / γγ [g] (as in 'go') άγκυρα anchor τσ [ts] (as in 'its') τσέπη pocket τζ [dz] (as in 'adds') τζάκι fireplace Word Construction Examples Follow these examples to see how sounds combine to form words. μα + μά = μαμά (mama / mother) πα + τέ + ρας = πατέρας (pateras / father) ου + ρα + νός = ουρανός (ouranos / sky) τζά + κι = τζάκι (tzaki / fireplace)

cases.title: Week 4: The Case System – Survival Scenario: Supermarket
cases.content: The Cases The role of a noun in a sentence is determined by its case . Modern Greek uses four primary cases. In the Supermarket, you'll mostly use the Nominative for items on your list and the Accusative for things you are buying. Survival Scenario: Supermarket (Στο Σούπερ Μάρκετ) Πού είναι το γάλα; (Where is the milk? - Nominative ) Θέλω ένα γάλα. (I want a milk. - Accusative ) Θέλω αυτόν τον χυμό. (I want this juice. - Accusative ) Έχετε φρούτα; (Do you have fruits?) Ονομαστική Nominative Role: The Subject Question: Who? / What? Ο Νίκος διαβάζει. Nick is reading. Γενική Genitive Role: Possession Question: Whose? Το χέρι του Νίκου . The hand of Nick . Αιτιατική Accusative Role: The Direct Object Question: Whom? / What? Βλέπω το(ν) Νίκο . I see Nick . Κλητική Vocative Role: Addressing Question: Hey! Who? Νίκο , πού είσαι; Nick , where are you? Summary of Word Changes Notice how the name Νίκος changed in each sentence: ο Νίκος (Nom) του Νίκου (Gen) τον Νίκο (Acc) Νίκο! (Voc)

neuter-declension.title: Week 7: Gender Focus – Neuter Declension
neuter-declension.content: Neuter Declension Neuter nouns in Greek are unique because the Nominative and Accusative cases are always identical in both singular and plural forms. They typically end in -ο , -ι , or -μα . 1. Nouns in -ο (το βιβλίο - the book) Case Singular Plural Nom το βιβλί ο τα βιβλί α Gen του βιβλί ου των βιβλί ων Acc το βιβλί ο τα βιβλί α 2. Nouns in -ι (το παιδί - the child) Case Singular Plural Nom το παιδ ί τα παιδ ιά Gen του παιδ ιού των παιδ ιών Acc το παιδ ί τα παιδ ιά 3. Nouns in -μα (το μάθημα - the lesson) These nouns add an extra syllable (-τα) in the plural. Case Singular Plural Nom το μάθη μα τα μαθή ματα Gen του μαθή ματος των μαθη μάτων Acc το μάθη μα τα μαθή ματα The Neuter Rule of Thumb In the neuter gender, the article for Nominative and Accusative is always the same: το in the singular and τα in the plural. This makes neuter nouns the easiest to use as objects!

definite-pronouns.title: Definite Pronouns
definite-pronouns.content: Definite Pronouns Definite pronouns in Greek are used to express identity ("the same") or exclusivity ("alone / by oneself"). ίδιος, ίδια, ίδιο The Same Always used with a definite article. Πήγα στον ίδιο γιατρό. I went to the same doctor.Έχουμε τα ίδια ρούχα. We have the same clothes. μόνος, μόνη, μόνο Alone / By oneself Often accompanied by a weak possessive pronoun (μου, σου...) to mean "by myself". Το τελείωσα μόνος μου . I finished it by myself . Vocabulary Note Be careful not to confuse the adjective μόνος (alone) with the adverb μόνο (only). Example: Ήμουν μόνος . (I was alone ) vs Έφαγα μόνο ένα. (I only ate one).

conjugation-present.title: Week 5: Present Tense – Daily Routines
conjugation-present.content: Present Conjugation Conjugation (Κλίση) involves changing the verb ending to match the person. Modern Greek verbs are grouped into four main paradigms. Group 1: Regular -ω (e.g. τρέχω) Εγώ τρέχ-ω Εσύ τρέχ-εις Αυτός/ή/ό τρέχ-ει Εμείς τρέχ-ουμε Εσείς τρέχ-ετε Αυτοί/ές/ά τρέχ-ουν Group 2: Accented -ώ (e.g. αγαπώ) Εγώ αγαπ-ώ Εσύ αγαπ-άς Αυτός/ή/ό αγαπ-ά Εμείς αγαπ-άμε Εσείς αγαπ-άτε Αυτοί/ές/ά αγαπ-ούν Group 3: Middle -ομαι (e.g. κάθομαι) Εγώ κάθ-ομαι Εσύ κάθ-εσαι Αυτός/ή/ό κάθ-εται Εμείς καθ-όμαστε Εσείς κάθ-εστε Αυτοί/ές/ά κάθ-ονται Group 4: Middle -άμαι (e.g. κοιμάμαι) Εγώ κοιμ-άμαι Εσύ κοιμ-άσαι Αυτός/ή/ό κοιμ-άται Εμείς κοιμ-όμαστε Εσείς κοιμ-άστε Αυτοί/ές/ά κοιμ-ούνται Alternative Endings In Modern Greek, many verbs have alternative endings in common speech. For example, αγαπ-άμε can also be αγαπ-ούμε , and τρέχ-ουν can be τρέχ-ουνε . Both are correct!

phrases.title: Week 2: Greetings – Survival Scenario: Ordering a Coffee
phrases.content: Essential Phrases Mastering these core phrases will help you navigate your first interactions in Greek. Practice saying them out loud to improve your pronunciation! Survival Scenario: Ordering a Coffee (Παραγγέλνοντας καφέ) In Greece, coffee is a social ritual. Use these phrases to get your fix! Έναν καφέ, παρακαλώ. (One coffee, please.) Ένα φρέντο εσπρέσο μέτριο. (One Freddo Espresso, medium sweetness.) Πόσο κάνει; (How much is it?) Ορίστε. (Here you go.) Greetings Γειά σου! Hello / Hi (informal) Γειά σας! Hello (formal/plural) Καλημέρα! Good morning! Καλησπέρα! Good evening! Basic Interaction Τι κάνεις; How are you? Καλά είμαι. I am well. Πώς σε λένε; What is your name? Με λένε... My name is... Courtesy Ευχαριστώ. Thank you. Παρακαλώ. Please / You're welcome. Συγγνώμη. I'm sorry / Excuse me. Ναι / Όχι Yes / No Did you know? The word Παρακαλώ is extremely versatile. It means "please", but also "you're welcome", and sometimes "I'm sorry?" or "Yes, can I help you?".

adverbs.title: Week 8: Manner – The Adverbs
adverbs.content: The Adverbs Adverbs (Επιρρήματα) are indeclinable words that provide more information about verbs, adjectives, or other adverbs. They tell us when , where , how , or how much something happened. Time (Χρονικά) σήμερα today αύριο tomorrow χτες yesterday τώρα now πάντα always ποτέ never Place (Τοπικά) εδώ here εκεί there πάνω up / on top κάτω down / below μέσα inside έξω outside Manner (Τροπικά) σιγά slowly / quietly γρήγορα quickly μαζί together έτσι like this ευτυχώς fortunately δυστυχώς unfortunately Quantity (Ποσοτικά) πολύ very / much λίγο a little αρκετά enough / quite καθόλου not at all Spelling Alert: πότε vs ποτέ Be careful with the accent! πότε (accent on the first syllable) means "when?" ποτέ (accent on the last syllable) means "never".

voices-moods.title: Week 12: Nuance – Voices and Moods
voices-moods.content: Voices and Moods Greek verbs have two Voices (who performs/receives the action) and several Moods (the attitude of the speaker towards the action). Voices (Φωνές) Ενεργητική (Active): The subject performs the action. Εγώ πλένω (I wash). Παθητική (Passive): The subject receives the action or acts on itself. Εγώ πλένομαι (I am being washed / I wash myself). Moods (Εγκλίσεις) Οριστική (Indicative): Facts and reality. Το παιδί παίζει (The child plays). Υποτακτική (Subjunctive): Wishes, possibilities, and "should". Often uses να . Θέλω να παίξω (I want to play). Προστακτική (Imperative): Commands and requests. Διάβασε! (Read!). Special Forms Greek also has Participles (Μετοχή) ending in -οντας (like "-ing" in English) and an Infinitive form (used in perfect tenses).

articles.title: Week 4: The Definite & Indefinite Articles
articles.content: The Articles In Greek, almost every noun is preceded by an article . The article tells us the gender, number, and case of the noun. There are two types: Definite ("the") and Indefinite ("a/an"). 1. The Definite Article (Το Οριστικό Άρθρο) This is used when referring to a specific item. In English, we only have "the", but in Greek, it changes based on gender. Gender Singular (The) Plural (The) Masculine ο οι Feminine η οι Neuter το τα Note: In the plural, Masculine and Feminine share the same article (οι), but Neuter uses τα. 2. The Indefinite Article (Το Αόριστο Άρθρο) This is used for non-specific items. There is no plural form for the indefinite article in Greek (similar to English—we don't say "a cars"). Gender Singular (A / An) Masculine ένας Feminine μία Neuter ένα Usage Examples ο πατέρας (the father) vs ένας πατέρας (a father) η μητέρα (the mother) vs μία μητέρα (a mother) το παιδί (the child) vs ένα παιδί (a child)

verb-groups.title: Groups of Verbs (Deep Dive)
verb-groups.content: Groups of Verbs In Greek, verbs are categorized into "Conjugations" (Συζυγίες) based on their ending in the Present Tense. Knowing which group a verb belongs to is essential for conjugating it correctly. First Conjugation -ω (unaccented)Verbs ending in -ω where the accent is on the stem. τρέχω (I run) παίζω (I play) Second Conjugation -ώ (accented)Verbs ending in -ώ where the accent is on the last syllable. αγαπώ (I love) νικώ (I win) Middle/Passive -όμαι / -άμαιVerbs ending in -μαι . These often have a reflexive or passive meaning. κάθομαι (I sit) κοιμάμαι (I sleep) Why the Accent Matters The accent mark on the final -ώ tells you that the verb follows a different set of endings (often adding an extra -α- in the conjugation). Example: 1st Group τρέχ-εις vs 2nd Group αγαπ-άς .
